chemicals.chemical_formula: Not explicitly provided in the sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ Foliage vs. Berries: While the plant produces black berries, the leaves (foliage) are explicitly cited as being more toxic than the berries. ◦ Severity: Toxicity is generally considered moderate.
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ Gastrointestinal Irritation: These saponins act as irritants to the gastrointestinal tract. Upon ingestion, they cause abdominal pain, excessive drooling (hypersalivation), vomiting, and diarrhea. ◦ Absorption: One source notes that the toxin is poorly absorbed upon ingestion, which typically limits the toxic effects to gastrointestinal issues rather than systemic organ failure. 4. Note
chemicals.name: Triterpenoid Saponins (specifically identified as Hederagenin and Hederin in the sources)

chemicals.chemical_formula: Not explicitly provided in the sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ Like the saponins, these are found within the plant material, with the foliage noted as the more toxic component compared to the berries.
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ These compounds work in conjunction with hederagenin to cause the clinical signs of toxicity, including vomiting, abdominal pain, and hypersalivation. 4. Notes on
chemicals.name: Polyacetylene compounds

description:
English Ivy (Hedera helix and related Hedera plants) is a climbing vine often grown indoors, in hanging baskets, or on outdoor walls. It is toxic to cats if chewed or eaten. The leaves are the main concern, and berries are also unsafe.

The plant contains triterpenoid saponins, including hederagenin, plus other irritating compounds. In plain terms, these chemicals can irritate the mouth and stomach, leading to drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, and belly pain. Some cats or people may also get skin irritation after repeated contact with the leaves or sap.

Most cases involve stomach upset, but a larger amount can cause weakness, wobbliness, or tremors. Keep indoor ivy and cuttings out of reach, and do not let cats chew ivy-covered walls or garden vines. If your cat bites or eats English ivy, remove the plant material and call a veterinarian for advice.
name: English Ivy
safetyNotes: Main risk: English ivy leaves contain triterpenoid saponins that irritate the mouth and stomach.
Symptoms: Drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, belly pain, and sometimes skin irritation after contact.
Higher concern: A larger amount may cause weakness, wobbliness, or tremors.
Action: Keep ivy plants and cuttings away from cats and call a vet if chewing or ingestion occurs.

symptoms.name: Vomiting, diarrhea, or belly pain
symptoms.onset: Usually soon after chewing or eating the plant.
symptoms.notes: These are the most common signs after English ivy ingestion.

symptoms.name: Drooling or mouth irritation
symptoms.onset: Often begins while chewing or shortly afterward.
symptoms.notes: Irritation can make the cat paw at the mouth or refuse food.

symptoms.name: Skin redness or itchiness after contact
symptoms.onset: After contact, especially in sensitive cats.
symptoms.notes: This is more likely with repeated contact with leaves or sap.

symptoms.name: Weakness, wobbliness, or tremors
symptoms.onset: Uncommon, but possible after larger ingestion.
symptoms.notes: Call a veterinarian promptly if these signs appear.

treatments.description: The immediate step is to prevent further ingestion and remove any remaining plant material from the cat's mouth. The mouth is then thoroughly rinsed with water to wash away the irritating sap and crystals that cause drooling and pain.
treatments.name: Oral Decontamination / Irrigation
treatments.notes: Because the toxins are poorly absorbed, systemic decontamination (like inducing vomiting) is not always necessary unless a large amount was ingested. Rinsing helps soothe the immediate burning sensation and oral irritation.Fluid Therapy and Electrolyte Correction

treatments.description: If the cat has experienced significant vomiting or diarrhea, the veterinarian will administer fluids to correct dehydration and restore electrolyte balance.
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) or Subcutaneous Fluid Therapy
treatments.notes: This is the primary treatment for cases showing clinical signs. Since the toxin primarily causes gastrointestinal upset (vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain), maintaining hydration is critical for recovery.Symptomatic Medication (Anti-emetics)

treatments.description: Veterinarians may administer medications to stop persistent vomiting (anti-emetics) and soothe the gastrointestinal tract (gastroprotectants).
treatments.name: Administration of Anti-emetics
treatments.notes: English Ivy toxicity is generally considered moderate and often self-limiting. Medication is typically reserved for cats causing significant distress or those at risk of severe dehydration from prolonged vomiting.
